title: Buttermilk pancakes
subtitle: Tall, fluffy American breakfast pancakes with maple syrup
excerpt: The American breakfast stack: thick, cloud-soft buttermilk pancakes cooked on a buttered griddle until the surface blisters with bubbles, flipped once, and served in a pile with butter and maple syrup. The buttermilk reacts with bicarbonate of soda to give the lift that makes these taller than a crêpe and softer than a pikelet.
sourceNotes: Buttermilk pancake recipes appear extensively in Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) and in Marion Harland's 'Common Sense in the Household' (1871, public domain). The combination of buttermilk with bicarbonate of soda as a leavener for griddle cakes is documented in American domestic literature from the mid-nineteenth century onward.

content.text: The chemistry is what makes an American buttermilk pancake different from a French crêpe or a British scotch pancake. Buttermilk is acidic; bicarbonate of soda is alkaline. When they combine in the batter, they produce carbon dioxide bubbles that inflate in the heat of the pan. The result is a thick, soft pancake with an open crumb, fluffy rather than chewy, with enough structure to absorb butter and maple syrup without going limp.

Two rules to remember: do not overmix the batter (a few visible lumps are correct and necessary), and rest the batter for 5 minutes before cooking to let the leavening activate. The pancakes are ready to flip when the surface is covered in dry-edged bubbles and the underside is golden, this takes 2 to 3 minutes per pancake. The second side cooks faster than the first.

items.name: Unsalted butter
items.prepNote: melted, plus extra for the pan

content.text: Whisk together the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} of bicarbonate of soda, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and a pinch of salt in a large bowl. In a jug, whisk together the {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk, the {{eggs}} eggs, and the {{unsalted-butter}} of melted butter. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and stir with a fork or spatula until just combined, the batter should be thick and lumpy. Stop mixing the moment the dry flour disappears. Leave to rest for 5 minutes.

Warm the cast-iron skillet over medium heat and add a small knob of butter, swirling to coat. When the butter stops foaming, ladle in about 80 ml of batter per pancake. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until bubbles break across the whole surface and the edges look dry and set. Flip once and cook for a further 1 to 2 minutes until golden. The second side will cook faster than the first. Keep warm in a low oven while you cook the remaining pancakes.

items.fix: Stir only until the flour disappears; lumps are correct and will vanish during cooking
items.cause: Batter overmixed; gluten developed and gas bubbles deflated

items.fix: Keep the heat at medium; American pancakes need a moderate heat to cook through before the crust over-browns
items.symptom: Pancakes are too dark outside but raw in the centre

items.fix: Add a small knob of butter for each batch and wait for it to stop foaming before adding the batter
items.cause: Not enough butter in the pan, or pan not at the right temperature

items.fix: Test your raising agents: drop a pinch of bicarbonate of soda in vinegar. It should fizz vigorously. Rest the batter for 5 minutes to allow activation
items.cause: Baking powder or bicarbonate of soda is old and has lost potency, or batter not rested

content.text: Griddle cakes made with buttermilk and bicarbonate of soda show up in American home cooking from the mid-1800s. By the late 1800s the American pancake breakfast (the tall stack with butter and maple syrup) was a fixture from New England diners to farmhouses in the Midwest. Maple syrup comes from the sap of sugar maple trees in the north-eastern United States and eastern Canada. It became the standard pairing as it spread from a local product to a national one over the 1900s. The soft, slightly tangy pancake and the round sweetness of maple are one of the great pairings in American cooking.
